speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] spilling--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I pick it up?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's fine. I'm running.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Linda [? Burr. ?] I'm interviewing Reuben Drehspul in Johannesburg, South Africa. It's October 16, 1995. Tape three. Reuben, can you tell us about that song that you just told us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This song was a song about the people who worked on the airport, and it prescribed the ingenuity how to survive. Wherever they used to stop, they tried to buy a potato, a piece of bread, or to change their clothing, or their own belongings for something else. It's a sad story. It's a sad song. Many people died on this aerodrome, which is airport. It was only a military airport, which was built or rebuilt by the Russian army in 1940, and it served the Northern Front, Russia. They used to come, the big planes. The [NON-ENGLISH], what flies around here in South Africa, was one of them. Three-engine planes, this was the biggest those days. And this song [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is two meanings. One, when they say, when  it sits on this track, and it stops, wherever it stops, it tried to buy food and to exchange clothing, maybe money too, if there was some money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and the other meaning was, if you belong to this brigade, and in a special brigade, you're lucky, you can survive sometimes. Because if you haven't got a job altogether, you don't stay a chance to surviving together. And many people lost their lives by beating them. When the Jewish women started to work there first on the airport, there were Russian prisoners who worked. And they used to come back and say stories that the Germans, the SS and even the Wehrmacht, used to chop off the Russian soldier's head for no reason at all, because it was a prisoner of war. And the work was very hard and very dangerous. First of all, they dealed with explosive things, with bombs or other stuff. And the Germans who were worked on the airport saw that a lot of invalids coming from the front line, and this beating them made them more aggressive and more hostile to the Jewish workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But life had to carry on. The Jewish nation must carry on, no matter what the suffering, and it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a matter of survival or dying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the-- what year was it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1943, when the Jewish people-- the younger-- established the underground, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be partisans, it was going on from mouth to mouth. They didn't advertise if you want to join the partisans or not. People knew each other, in particular from age group 21 till 30, they took in to be trained somewhere in the forests to be partisans and to try to destroy the German's lines-- the railroad lines, whatever they could to sabotage, to blow up factories and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans knew that there was Jewish partisans. When I said before the [? English ?] guy actually was a partisan. And the Jewish people were very careful to deal with anything. And the civilian population, the non-Jewish people, the Aryan side, stopped dealing with the Jewish or to supply them with food and so on. And a lot of the Lithuanian population-- not the Jewish, outside the ghetto-- lived for Germany. They volunteered to work in Germany. In the ghetto, life was very bad. And people felt there was no medication, there was no food. And on the black market, we could still buy saccharine, means sweetener, flour, bacon, eggs, chickens. And as I said, my cousin, she used to be a chicken smuggler. Why she smuggled it to life, I don't know. Maybe she picked it up on the road. [LAUGHS] I remember, later on, I'll tell you about myself in Germany. And people start to build bunkers in the ghetto. As I mentioned before, one bunker, this policemen didn't even know his own wife and child was in the bunker, because people didn't discuss anything between themselves unless it was maybe very close family. And this was not a single case, not one case, it was several cases. The Jewish police, or Jewish firemen, showed the Germans where the hiders are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish people, in 1943, start to build better bunkers, more sophisticated, and they installed air ventilation pipes, and food supplies, and water supplies, if possible. I'm telling this because I come to the point later on I'd been in one of those.

speaker: interviewee
text: But people got very weak. You couldn't buy medication, not even a bandage, not even a dentist. If you  had trouble with a tooth, take an ordinary plier and pull it out. To go to the hospital, we knew it was dangerous, that they blow up the small ghetto hospital with the doctors, with the nurses, with the sisters, with the children. It was unbearable, too terrible. I saw it with my eyes. I couldn't believe in the beginning. And two days later, when it was settled and took stock of ourselves, we realized what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, was there any religious life in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, by the way, I had my Bar Mitzvah in the camp, in the ghetto. We wore ordinary clothing, not like in a concentration camp. Clothing was expensive. Some people came from outskirts of Kovno, came with nothing. It was the Jewish people who helped the Jewish people. I, actually, when I moved into the ghetto the first time, and they made the ghetto smaller, I was accommodated to sleep in the synagogue, in Slobodka synagogue. And still very religious Jewish people with beards, although they had to shave their beards, otherwise the Germans pulled them by their beards, cut off with scissors, give them a hiding, murder them, kick them, but religious life went on. There used to be minyonem every morning, 4 o'clock in the morning, to daven. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and whatever it is [INAUDIBLE]. And I had a great uncle named Irs Miller. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He used to teach me my [NON-ENGLISH] for my Bar Mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when the day came in, my Bar Mitzvah, I had to remember mitzvah [NON-ENGLISH] 5 o'clock in the morning, we made my service, and it's a [INAUDIBLE] part of me. 6 o'clock, the men had to go to work, and from nowhere the Germans walk into the synagogue and shouted, everybody, [SPEAKING GERMAN], get out, get out, get out. I, with my great uncle Irs, went-- we walked to my mother's place. And we came to  our apartment in the block, in the block, in the block of flats. And she took out a piece of bread for me and for my great uncle, Irs Miller, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and she spoke to me. She said, according to the Jewish Bible, today you became a man. And she gave me a piece of bread, maybe milk or some other thing to drink, and she blessed me. And  my-- Irs Miller blessed me too, and he say, [NON-ENGLISH], and he went back to his flat or house. I can't remember where he stayed. And this was my Bar Mitzvah. I had no party, I had no musical instruments going on, only fear. Fear from the Germans, fear from hunger, and fear from disease. Fear that nobody can put it in words and writing. It's a feeling. It's like the world falls on you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's--people asked me after the war, how did you feel? I say, I can't describe-- I can't explain to you how you feel. It's most terrible thing, I suppose. Even a person when he dies, he comes to his place when he doesn't know already that he dies.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's terrible. People used to die, just die in the street, die in the flat. Used to go around, the cart, every morning in the ghetto and pick up the dead people, from here, from there, and so on. And the ghetto still gets smaller. They still take away people for-- for liquidation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in this Jewish hospital, I was told, that the doctors were very good doctors. Actually, one of the doctors, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], was a eye specialist, and I knew him before the war. And then we came to Germany, he was in the same camp with me. And people tried-- tried to give their children what they still had in the ghetto to the peasants of Lithuania, which a few did, maybe a few dozens, gave their children for money, for gold to hide out. And I know cases that after the war, the non-Jewish people who hide out, who brought up the children, didn't want to part with the children, because they got used to them. But some people managed. I actually met one survivor from the ghetto. I met her in Johannesburg. And her father went back to Lithuania, to the [NON-ENGLISH], or the village, and with some tricks he got his daughter out. And she came to South Africa, and she survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But a lot of children were handed over to the non-Jewish people who say they'll look after them, they'll fed them, they'll clothe them, for money, for everything. The next day, they gave them over to the Germans, and the children were liquidated. Because the-- the Christian people were not good to the-- the civilian population of Lithuania, the Lithuanians themselves, were a very bad nation. Although, I, by myself, this is my opinion. I can't judge people, but that's what I think in my mind. And people used to die a lot. We had a burial place somewhere. I can't remember. It's many years ago. They used to be buried. Used to put them in a cart on two wheels or four wheels, take them to the cemetery, bury them, and finished. And the world still went on. And I became a man. I became 13 years of age. That's a big thing in Jewish religion. And the Jewish religious life, religious people went on. They used to daven whenever possible. They used to wear [NON-ENGLISH]. Even in my Bar Mitzvah, when I [INAUDIBLE] people put on [NON-ENGLISH] for what reason or other one, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE] was a kosher. Jewish people, especially the religious path was-- they say Litha is like Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Very religious people, kind people. Although they say in every nation they are kind people, but there are nations which is not kind. I was asked after the war, was there any good Germans? I say, yes, many of them. Dead ones. This is my opinion about them. Reuben, how long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, I was-- I was from the last one before they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They liquidated the ghetto in 1944, and it was terrible to see when the Germans went from house to house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The demolition squads, in particular, put dynamite and blew up the house. They didn't even went into the house. They just dynamited the house and blew it up, whatever is there. If it were people or not, they just blew up. As a matter of fact, my late uncle and auntie, [NON-ENGLISH] from my father's side, and his wife and his two children in the bunker, a hideout under the floor in the house, and the mother, and they were burned alive, or blew up alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they blew up house to house for days. And we saw things are banding together. And my father was running around on the roof, and, look, they blow up this and this, and they're coming closer to us. What can we do? We got nowhere to hide ourselves. So he find other people moving to a certain direction, certain people who were wealthy people in our block where we've stayed. And you find out that in this [NON-ENGLISH], this place where they used to de-louse the people, used to be showers steam, used to kill the lice, [INAUDIBLE], whatever it is, people going in through the oven with a prearranged [NON-ENGLISH], a hideout place. So he come to us and says, let's go, we find a hideout place. So we come, but they wouldn't let us in. Say, you got gold, you got diamonds, you got money. He says, I got nothing. I got my life. If you don't let us in, you have no your life and not our life, because the Germans will find us any minute here. It's a matter of 10 or 15 minutes the Germans will be us, and you will not manage to close the doors, because we're going to stay here. It's a matter of life and death for you and for us. So they went in there to discuss, the elders, and they let us in.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a dig out place under the foundations of this big block, and the insulation was very bad. It was built for 60 or 70 people, and it must have been 100 people. You couldn't stand up. You had to crawl into this place, and it was very hot. And we were the first night and you could hear explosions, blowing up buildings, shaking. You could hear the Germans. And in the place where I was lying was a little window, but digged into the ground like a basement window, and this was the air was coming in. And all of a sudden, I see a little box with two wires is pushed into this hole where the window's supposed to be or whatever it is. And I say to my father-- alive then-- I think, dad, they're putting in a box of dynamite, electric one. He looks at this, he say, you're quite right. It's dynamite-- electric bomb. He said, you only have to press a button and it'll explode.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we still lying there a day and a night, and the explosions everywhere. The demolition squad still didn't manage to come to us, but they put in the bombs wherever the Germans felt, and then the other guys, the other Germans used to come, they detonate and blow it up. So another night went through, and the explosions from house to house. We didn't hear any noises, only explosions. And we were not allowed to go out from this place, because it's dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was a heavy smoker. I also was a heavy smoker, thanks to the American army who gave me cigarettes in 1945. [INAUDIBLE] So my father tried to-- tried these matches to light his cigarettes, and it just became blue but it didn't light. And so  a woman, maybe my [INAUDIBLE] tried to light the match. So I realized, I said, Pa, the match will not light. We haven't got enough oxygen. I can see it comes with blue, the chemical of the match burns off but doesn't light. There's no air. And everybody was perspiring, and it was so hot. The women were sitting half-naked, excuse me, we were sitting naked or half-naked. There was no food left, no water, nothing. So there's a meeting, the guys in charge, that we should go back through the oven and sit down the benches-- not benches-- benches, bunks, before you get undressed, to sit down and catch fresh air, and we'll come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they opened the doors and the oven door, and we went through the oven. And we're sitting down and around, around, the 80 people was in the bunker, and we're catching fresh air. And you could hear the explosions, terrible explosions. Everything is shaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in a concrete building, which the Russians built. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one woman couldn't get out, elderly woman, let her rest in peace. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. Correct. Now I remember the name. And my father and other people say, Mrs. Vinacur, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], please, come out, get some fresh air, there's no air, you'll die like this. She wouldn't come out, she wouldn't go out, and you couldn't just put her out. I think we sat for half an hour or 20 minutes, and all of a sudden, the door was broken in by two Germans. One was a officer, other one was a soldier from the SS special unit. Demolition squad on top of it. He walks in, the lights was on-- or not. I can't remember. Sorry, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, what you doing here? Can't you see that we're blowing up the ghetto? What you're sitting here? And he say to me, you, boy, switch off the lights. Now I remember, it was lights. So I went up to the switch, and I switched off the light, and I faced the German, and he came closer to me, and he took out his gun. And I can-- now I know what gun it was, it was a Mauser, a special German Mauser gun, handgun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took his gun and put at my forehead, and I looked him in his eyes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I looked him in his eyes and he looked me, and his gun is facing against my temple. And he pulled back his gun and he say to me, junger and everybody, get out of here. We're blowing up the building any minute. And we walked out this building. My late mother couldn't walk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother then was still alive. My sister was subconscious, and a other few women. We pulled them out to the street, they got some air, and the Germans said, anybody who can walk to the police station must walk with us to the police station. The people who can't walk must walk to the hospital, or it'll come, people walk around with carts, and they'll take you to the hospital. My sister was revived. She was all right. My father was all right. I, myself, was-- thanks God, I think I was all right. My mother, let her rest in peace, she's dead now, but that day she was alive, but she was unconscious. And it came a cart, and they took away some people to the hospital. I, personally, myself, thought they take us somewhere else, but they took her to the hospital. And we were four days in the yard of the police station with no food, water [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and the ghetto was blowing up to pieces. Smoke, fire, terribly. And they took out all the people from the ghetto through the main gate to-- and they had to march six kilometers to a railway siding. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The police told us, they're marching and they were transported somewhere by rail. And-- and the third day, the Germans came to us, and they told us we must march three in a row, and we marched to the siding. And we come to the siding, and there were trains, cattle cars waiting for us. Cattle trains, whatever you call it, [NON-ENGLISH], and we were pushed into those cattle trains. And the ghetto was blowing up to pieces. Nobody remained there. People were in hideouts. If we wouldn't-- If we wouldn't go out for fresh air from our hideout, we would've most likely blown up to pieces, because there was dynamite in this particular windowsill and it would've blown up. And this was the end of the ghetto. People marched to the trains, and they were transported to Poland and to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [DEEP  EXHALE]
